content.text: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) is a neural network architecture that replaces each dense feed-forward layer with a collection of

that selects a small subset of experts for each token. The result is a model whose total parameter count scales independently of its per-token computation budget: a 100-billion-parameter MoE can compute with the same FLOP cost as a 10-billion-parameter dense model while benefitting from the richer representations stored across all experts.

This sub-subtopic explains how expert sub-networks are structured, how the router works mathematically, what load-balancing losses prevent expert collapse, and what MoE means for training and inference.

attrs.alt: MoE layer: router scores 6 experts, selects top-2, computes weighted sum of their outputs

content.text: In a standard Transformer, each layer contains a multi-head self-attention block followed by a dense feed-forward network (FFN). In a MoE model, the single FFN is replaced by
expert FFNs plus a router. Everything else — attention, layer norms, residual connections — is unchanged.
is itself a standard two-layer FFN:
are the expert's own weight matrices, entirely independent of every other expert.
The router is a small learned linear projection that maps the token hidden state
to a distribution over experts. Given
is the model hidden dimension. For Top-
largest logits are kept; the rest are masked to
The MoE layer output is the weighted sum of selected expert outputs:
Higher recall; diminishing returns on quality

Mixtral 8x7B uses 8 experts per layer with K=2: each token routes through exactly 2 of the 8 experts. Active parameters per token are equivalent to a ~12.9B dense model, while total parameters are ~46.7B — providing the representational capacity of a much larger model.
Without any constraint, the router learns to prefer one or two experts and starve the rest —
. This is catastrophic: the ignored experts never receive gradients and effectively disappear. Two mechanisms counteract it.
Add an auxiliary loss that penalises unequal expert utilisation. Let
be the fraction of tokens routed to expert
the average router probability assigned to expert
is minimised when utilisation is uniform.
is a small coefficient (≈ 0.01) so the auxiliary loss does not dominate the main task loss.
C that limits how many tokens any single expert may process per batch:
is the total number of tokens in the batch. If an expert is over-capacity, excess tokens are passed through unchanged (
). C = 1.25 is a common default: experts can handle 25% more than their fair share before capping.
During training, adding Gaussian noise to the router logits before the top-K selection prevents the router from converging prematurely to a fixed expert assignment:
Noise is typically scaled by a learnable parameter and is disabled at inference time.
Mixtral 8x7B places a MoE layer at every Transformer block. Its configuration:

At inference the model loads all 46.7B weights into GPU memory (or CPU+GPU with offloading) but computes only 12.9B parameters' worth of FLOPs per forward pass. This makes it faster than a dense 46.7B model while matching or exceeding a dense 13B model on benchmarks.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

class MoELayer(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, d_model: int, d_ff: int, num_experts: int, top_k: int):
        super().__init__()
        self.num_experts = num_experts
        self.top_k = top_k
        self.router = nn.Linear(d_model, num_experts, bias=False)
        # Each expert is an independent FFN
        self.experts = nn.ModuleList([
            nn.Sequential(
                nn.Linear(d_model, d_ff),
                nn.GELU(),
                nn.Linear(d_ff, d_model),
            )
            for _ in range(num_experts)
        ])

    def forward(self, x: torch.Tensor) -> tuple[torch.Tensor, torch.Tensor]:
        # x: [batch, seq_len, d_model]
        B, T, D = x.shape
        x_flat = x.view(-1, D)              # [B*T, d_model]

        # Router scores
        logits = self.router(x_flat)        # [B*T, E]
        scores = F.softmax(logits, dim=-1)  # [B*T, E]

        # Top-K selection
        top_scores, top_idx = scores.topk(self.top_k, dim=-1)  # [B*T, K]
        top_scores = top_scores / top_scores.sum(dim=-1, keepdim=True)  # renormalise

        # Compute expert outputs and weighted sum
        out = torch.zeros_like(x_flat)
        for k in range(self.top_k):
            expert_ids = top_idx[:, k]   # which expert for position k
            weights    = top_scores[:, k]  # gating weight
            for e in range(self.num_experts):
                mask = (expert_ids == e)
                if mask.any():
                    out[mask] += weights[mask, None] * self.experts[e](x_flat[mask])

        # Auxiliary load-balancing loss
        f = torch.zeros(self.num_experts, device=x.device)
        for e in range(self.num_experts):
            f[e] = (top_idx == e).float().mean()
        P = scores.mean(dim=0)
        aux_loss = self.num_experts * (f * P).sum()

        return out.view(B, T, D), aux_loss

the nested loop above is for clarity, not speed. Production MoE implementations (Megablocks, DeepSpeed-MoE) use custom CUDA kernels that batch all tokens for the same expert together with a single matrix multiply, achieving near-dense throughput.
mitigated by auxiliary load-balancing loss and router noise.
in data-parallel training, tokens for the same expert may reside on different GPUs, requiring all-to-all communication each layer.
hard Top-1 routing is non-differentiable at the boundary; Top-2 with softmax weights is smoother and more stable.
capacity factor C trades dropped tokens (too low) against wasted compute on padding (too high). Sweep on a validation split.
A natural question is whether MoE experts genuinely learn distinct skills or whether they simply become redundant copies of each other. Empirical analysis of trained MoE models shows that routing is
and correlates with linguistically meaningful categories.
Studies on GShard and Switch-Base reveal consistent biases:
Factual recall concentrated in specific experts
These patterns emerge without any explicit supervision: the auxiliary load-balancing loss prevents collapse but does not prescribe
expert handles which token type. The specialisation arises purely from gradient descent.
A simple diagnostic is to collect routing decisions on a held-out corpus and compute the per-expert frequency distribution over token categories:

import collections
import torch

def profile_routing(model, dataloader, tokenizer, layer_idx=0):
    """Collect per-expert token-category frequencies for one MoE layer."""
    counts = collections.defaultdict(lambda: collections.Counter())

    model.eval()
    with torch.no_grad():
        for batch in dataloader:
            input_ids = batch["input_ids"]
            # Hook into the layer's router to capture gate indices
            hook_data = {}
            def hook(module, inp, out):
                # out: (output, indices) where indices shape [T, K]
                hook_data["indices"] = out[1].cpu()

            handle = model.layers[layer_idx].moe.register_forward_hook(hook)
            model(**batch)
            handle.remove()

            indices = hook_data["indices"]  # [T, K]
            tokens  = input_ids.view(-1)    # [T]
            for t_idx, expert_set in enumerate(indices):
                token_str = tokenizer.decode([tokens[t_idx]])
                for e in expert_set.tolist():
                    counts[e][token_str] += 1

    return {e: ctr.most_common(10) for e, ctr in counts.items()}

Expert routing distributions are statistically non-uniform (KL divergence from uniform ≈ 0.3–0.8 nats in trained models vs ≈ 0 in randomly initialised models), confirming that specialisation develops during training.
The most significant engineering challenge in MoE is that experts are sharded across GPUs — each GPU holds a subset of experts. Routing a token to expert i may therefore require a
, turning the MoE layer into a communication-intensive collective operation.
(EP): distribute the E experts evenly across EP GPUs. For a model with 8 experts and EP = 4, each GPU holds 2 experts. The forward pass of a MoE layer then requires:
Each GPU broadcasts its locally-computed gate decisions and ships the corresponding token embeddings to whichever GPU holds the selected expert.
Each GPU applies its 2 local experts to the tokens it received.
Each GPU ships expert outputs back to the originating GPU, which accumulates the weighted sum.

import torch.distributed as dist

def dispatch_and_gather(x, gate_indices, gate_weights, num_experts, ep_group):
    """
    Simplified all-to-all dispatch for expert parallelism.
    x:             [T, d]  — token embeddings on this rank
    gate_indices:  [T, K]  — which experts each token chose
    gate_weights:  [T, K]  — softmax weights
    ep_group:       ProcessGroup for expert-parallel ranks
    """
    ep_size = dist.get_world_size(ep_group)
    experts_per_rank = num_experts // ep_size

    # --- 1. Build send buffers: partition tokens by destination rank ---
    send_tensors = [[] for _ in range(ep_size)]
    token_metadata = []  # (token_idx, k_slot, weight, dest_rank, local_expert_id)

    for t in range(x.size(0)):
        for k in range(gate_indices.size(1)):
            expert_id  = gate_indices[t, k].item()
            dest_rank  = expert_id // experts_per_rank
            local_exp  = expert_id  % experts_per_rank
            weight     = gate_weights[t, k].item()
            send_tensors[dest_rank].append(x[t])
            token_metadata.append((t, k, weight, dest_rank, local_exp))

    # --- 2. All-to-all: exchange token counts, then token data ---
    send_counts = torch.tensor([len(s) for s in send_tensors], dtype=torch.long)
    recv_counts = torch.zeros_like(send_counts)
    dist.all_to_all_single(recv_counts, send_counts, group=ep_group)

    send_buf = torch.cat(send_tensors, dim=0) if send_tensors else x.new_empty(0, x.size(1))
    recv_buf = torch.empty(recv_counts.sum().item(), x.size(1), device=x.device, dtype=x.dtype)
    dist.all_to_all_single(recv_buf, send_buf,
                           output_split_sizes=recv_counts.tolist(),
                           input_split_sizes=send_counts.tolist(),
                           group=ep_group)

    return recv_buf, recv_counts, token_metadata

All-to-all latency grows linearly with expert count and inversely with inter-GPU bandwidth. For large clusters, this communication overhead can consume 20–40% of total training time. Techniques such as
(grouping multiple tokens destined for the same expert into a single GEMM call) and
(overlapping communication with local attention computation) reduce this overhead substantially.
Production serving of MoE models presents unique challenges absent from dense model deployment. The key levers are:

Because tokens in a forward pass tend to activate the same small subset of experts, it is often possible to keep the most frequently used experts in fast GPU SRAM while spilling less-used experts to slower DRAM or even NVMe.
demonstrated that in large LLMs, ~97% of tokens are handled by a predictable ≤5% of experts, enabling near-lossless expert caching with a tiny predictor head.

Unlike dense models where any batch size yields proportional GPU utilisation, sparse routing means that at small batch sizes some experts may receive zero tokens — wasting their GPU capacity. Practical inference systems aggregate requests across users to maintain enough tokens per batch that all experts receive work:

Expert weight matrices are large: for Mixtral 8x7B, each of the 32 layers has 8 × 2 weight matrices of shape (4096, 14336) and (14336, 4096). At bf16 this is ~28 GB just for expert weights. Common inference strategies:
Reduces expert memory from 28 GB → ~7 GB with minimal perplexity penalty (<0.3 ppl)
Keep active-layer experts on GPU, stream next-layer experts from CPU RAM during current-layer compute
Split each expert's weight matrix across multiple GPUs using column/row parallel linear layers
The headline claim is that MoE achieves better quality at the same FLOP budget as a dense model. This is empirically supported across scale:

The trade-off is clear: MoE wins on quality-per-FLOP and quality-per-parameter but loses on serving cost (memory and inter-GPU bandwidth). This makes MoE most attractive when training compute is the binding constraint, and serving can be handled by high-memory multi-GPU setups.
A MoE layer replaces one dense FFN with E independent expert FFNs and a router that selects the Top-K experts per token.
The router is a learned linear projection followed by softmax; gating weights are used to form a weighted sum of expert outputs.
and expert capacity limits prevent expert collapse.
MoE models decouple total parameter count from per-token FLOPs: Mixtral 8x7B activates ~12.9B params per token despite having ~46.7B total.
The main inference cost is memory: all expert weights must be loaded regardless of how many are active per token.
